verdicts.note: The record claims exists='yes' and points to https://github.com/helpscout/helpscout-api-php. However, this is Help Scout's official PHP SDK, NOT a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server. The evidence shows no dedicated MCP server. The 'flags' mention a community npm 'help-scout-mcp-server' but no evidence URL validates this, and the provided URL is definitively not an MCP server.
The URL https://github.com/helpscout/helpscout-api-php points to Help Scout's PHP SDK for the Mailbox API, not an MCP server. This should be removed.

Evidence confirms OAuth2 (Authorization Code & Client Credentials) for Mailbox API, API key via HTTP Basic Auth for Docs API, and JWT-based callback for restricted Docs. All three auth methods are substantiated.

Pricing page confirms Free plan exists but the evidence states 'API access' is reserved for paid plans (Plus and above). OAuth app creation and Docs API key generation are self-service within the account UI for paid users, matching 'paid-plan'.

Evidence confirms API access is not available on the Free plan and that OAuth app creation (My apps) and Docs API key generation are self-service for users on paid plans. Docs API also requires 'Docs: Create new, edit settings & Collections' permission from an admin.
Evidence shows Mailbox API v2 and Docs API v1 are both REST APIs (HTTPS endpoints). No GraphQL mentioned.

Mailbox API covers conversations, customers, users, inboxes, etc.; Docs API covers collections/articles. 'Medium' breadth is reasonable given two separate REST APIs covering helpdesk and help-site domains.
'buildable-with-caveats' is appropriate: the evidence confirms API access requires a paid plan (Plus or higher) and OAuth credentials must be tied to an active invited user, creating setup friction.
Evidence directly supports that API access requires a paid plan (Plus or higher) and OAuth2 app credentials must be associated with an active invited user.
All four evidence URLs are substantiated by the provided content. Each URL supports the claims in the 'supports' field.

The first flag mentions 'a community/third-party npm package (help-scout-mcp-server)' but no evidence is provided for this. The existing_mcp.url points to a PHP SDK, not an MCP server. The other two flags (paid plan requirement, two distinct APIs) are supported by evidence.

revised.name: Help Scout
revised.one_liner: Help Scout offers a mature REST Mailbox API (OAuth2) and a separate Docs API (API key/Basic auth) for helpdesk data, gated behind paid plans, with a community MCP server already available.

auth_methods.notes: Mailbox API v2 uses OAuth2 Authorization Code (for third-party integrations) and Client Credentials (for internal integrations) flows. Requires creating an OAuth2 app under Your Profile > My apps to get client id/secret. All requests need Authorization: Bearer header.

Separate Docs API uses per-user API keys passed via HTTP Basic Auth (API key as username, dummy password). Requires 'Docs: Create new, edit settings & Collections' permission granted by account owner/admin.
Restricted Docs sites use a custom JWT-based callback authentication flow for embedding restricted help content.

revised.self_serve_notes: API access (Mailbox API) is not available on Help Scout's Free plan; developers must upgrade to at least the Plus plan to use the API. OAuth app creation and Docs API key generation are self-service within the account UI once on a qualifying paid plan, but require an active invited user and (for Docs API) specific permission granted by an admin.

api_surface.notes: Mailbox API v2 covers conversations, customers, users, inboxes, organizations, etc. Docs API v1 covers help-site collections/articles separately. No GraphQL; SDKs exist (e.g., PHP) but core surface is REST.

revised.main_blocker: API access requires a paid Help Scout plan (Plus or higher), and OAuth2 app credentials must be tied to an active invited user, adding setup friction beyond simple self-serve API key issuance.

evidence.supports: OAuth2 Authorization Code and Client Credentials flows, Bearer token auth, OAuth2 app creation via My Apps
Docs API uses per-user API key via HTTP Basic Auth, permission requirements, rate limits
Restricted Docs sites use custom JWT-based callback authentication
API access reserved for paid plans (Plus and above), not available on Free plan

flags: Paid plan requirement (Plus tier) for API access may limit self-serve onboarding for trial/free-tier agent use cases.
Two distinct APIs (Mailbox OAuth2 vs Docs API key) with different auth mechanisms increases integration complexity for a unified toolkit.
